spoken_input:
The Commission traced a history. Tests and flights had shown O-ring damage. Hot gases were not supposed to touch the O-rings, yet flight experience showed that the seal system was not behaving with the margin the program wanted to believe it had.

STS-2 was an early receipt. After that flight, inspection found primary O-ring erosion in the right Solid Rocket Booster aft field joint. Hot motor gas had reached a seal that was supposed to be protected from it.

That should have carried force.

Instead, the anomaly did not reach the Level I Flight Readiness Review for STS-3. It was not tracked in the Marshall problem assessment system in the way the later record would make necessary.

The warning existed at a level where the system could continue.

The program also had criticality language. The field-joint seal had been treated as redundant, then later shifted after the secondary O-ring could no longer be assumed to provide reliable backup under all conditions. The administrative label changed, but flight continued.

That paperwork trail matters. The system had a way to name catastrophic risk. It had a way to say that a failure could mean loss of vehicle and crew. But naming criticality did not automatically force the launch process to behave as if every warning around that part was critical.

The label and the practice began to separate.

The paperwork made the danger more visible. The launch process still made it manageable. A technical fact can be documented accurately and still fail to become a launch-stopping fact.

In the Shuttle program, repeated damage should have sharpened attention. Repeated survival also created a dangerous kind of confidence.

STS 51-C increased concern. The flight showed hot gas past the primary O-ring, and low temperature was part of the discussion. Thiokol concluded that the condition was "not desirable but acceptable."

That phrase matters because it sounds disciplined. It sounds like risk has been bounded. But if the boundary is wrong, the phrase becomes a way to keep flying with a condition that should be getting harder to accept.

Then came STS 51-B. After primary O-ring erosion, a launch constraint was placed. A launch constraint should be a hard object in the path. It should say: resolve this before flight.

But the constraint was waived for later flights, including the path toward 51-L.

previous_text:
uickly enough to keep combustion gas inside the motor.

The Commission found that cold reduced O-ring resiliency. A compressed O-ring at 75 degrees Fahrenheit could return toward its shape far faster than one at 30 degrees Fahrenheit. Challenger's right aft field joint was not merely cold in the ordinary sense. It was estimated near 28 degrees Fahrenheit, with a five-degree uncertainty on either side.

The seal did not need to disappear.

It only needed to be late.

The exact temperature matters because "cold" can sound like background weather. Here, cold was part of the mechanism. It changed the response time of a part that was already being asked to seal while the joint was opening.

That timing problem matters because it connects the physical failure to the organizational one. The hardware needed to respond before combustion escaped the joint. The organization needed to respond before repeated evidence became routine.

The joint problem was not discovered cleanly after the disaster.
next_text:
That is where formal safety language can become misleading. The record can still say "constraint" while the operating habit says "waiver." Once a constraint has been waived, the next waiver is easier to imagine. The first exception becomes part of the experience base.

Each waiver changed the practical meaning of the constraint. The words still existed. The stopping power weakened.

The constraint history also keeps the story from becoming one bad meeting. The system had already built a way to keep the joint issue compatible with flight. By the time January 27 arrived, launch was not pushing against a fresh warning. It was pushing against a warning that had already been processed several times.

In July 1985, Roger Boisjoly warned that a similar field-joint problem could lead to a catastrophe of the highest order, including loss of human life. That memo did not come from hindsight. It came from inside the system, months before Challenger.
